name: Nussbaum, Maury A.
recent_1: Rajabi, M. S., Ojelade, A., Kim, S., & Nussbaum, M. A. (2026). Vision-language models for occupational physical exposure assessment: Classification and temporal segmentation of manual material handling tasks. Applied Ergonomics, 124, 104831. DOI: 10.1016/j.apergo.2026.104831
recent_2: Akinwande, F., Kim, S., & Nussbaum, M. A. (2026). Passive back- and arm-support exoskeletons have effects on physical demands and user perceptions in simulated manual mining tasks that are generally beneficial but are both device- and task-specific. Applied Ergonomics, 122, 104819. DOI: 10.1016/j.apergo.2026.104819
recent_3: Choi, J., Kim, S., Lim, S., Porto, R., & Nussbaum, M. A. (2026). Effectiveness and usability of a trunk posture feedback system: A longitudinal field study for up to six weeks in a distribution center. Applied Ergonomics, 121, 104796. DOI: 10.1016/j.apergo.2026.104796
recent_1_synopsis: This study applied vision-language models to ordinary video of manual material handling, both classifying task types and temporally segmenting work cycles to support automated occupational physical exposure assessment. It advances scalable, camera-based ergonomic risk evaluation by leveraging modern multimodal AI in place of manual observation or instrumented sensing.
recent_2_synopsis: This field-style biomechanical evaluation tested passive back-support and arm-support exoskeletons during simulated manual mining tasks, quantifying their effects on physical demands and worker perceptions. It found that benefits are generally present but strongly device- and task-specific, informing whether and where exoskeletons should be deployed in heavy, unstructured industrial work.
recent_3_synopsis: This longitudinal field study deployed a wearable trunk-posture feedback system to distribution-center workers for up to six weeks, assessing both its effectiveness at reducing risky trunk postures and its real-world usability over time. It provides rare long-duration field evidence on whether real-time posture feedback produces durable behavior change outside the laboratory.
seminal_1: Barker, L. M., & Nussbaum, M. A. (2011). Fatigue, performance and the work environment: a survey of registered nurses. Journal of Advanced Nursing, 67(6), 1370-1382. DOI: 10.1111/j.1365-2648.2010.05597.x
seminal_2: Lin, D., Seol, H., Nussbaum, M. A., & Madigan, M. L. (2008). Reliability of COP-based postural sway measures and age-related differences. Gait & Posture, 28(2), 337-342. DOI: 10.1016/j.gaitpost.2008.01.005
seminal_1_synopsis: This large survey of registered nurses characterized physical, mental, and total fatigue and linked them to work-environment factors and self-reported performance, providing one of the field's most-used datasets on occupational fatigue in healthcare. With roughly 657 citations (Google Scholar, 2026-06-17) it is Nussbaum's most-cited work and a foundational reference connecting work-system design to fatigue and patient-safety-relevant performance.
seminal_2_synopsis: This study quantified the test-retest reliability of center-of-pressure (COP) based postural sway measures and documented age-related differences in balance, establishing which sway metrics are dependable for research on balance and fall risk. With roughly 630 citations (Google Scholar, 2026-06-17) it is a foundational methodological reference widely used in postural-control, aging, and fall-prevention research.
expertise_overview: Nussbaum studies occupational biomechanics, work physiology, and ergonomics with the central aim of understanding and preventing work-related musculoskeletal disorders while improving worker performance and well-being. Core threads include muscle fatigue measurement and modeling, low-back and trunk biomechanics during lifting and manual material handling, and balance, postural control, and fall risk, especially across the aging workforce. A major recent emphasis is the biomechanical and perceptual evaluation of occupational exoskeletons (back-support, arm-support, and upper-extremity devices) across diverse industrial tasks such as assembly, mining, construction, and overhead work. He also develops wearable-sensor and computer-vision/AI methods, including vision-language models and posture-feedback systems, for scalable physical exposure assessment and real-time ergonomic intervention. His methods span laboratory biomechanics and EMG, longitudinal field studies, and quantitative and data-driven modeling, often integrating physical and mental workload. Application domains include manufacturing and automotive assembly, mining and construction, healthcare and nursing, and distribution/warehousing work.
notes: Identity confirmed with high confidence as Maury A. Nussbaum, Virginia Tech, Grado Department of Industrial and Systems Engineering (Occupational Ergonomics and Biomechanics Laboratory). Confirmed via Google Scholar profile (user=XMRtFS8AAAAJ; ~16,208 total citations, h-index 67, i10-index 239), the Virginia Tech ISE faculty page, his personal CV site, Virginia Tech experts/Elements page, and ResearchGate, plus consistency with the pre-verified Human Factors hf_papers (exoskeleton/balance themes; recurring collaborator Sunwook Kim). All five cited DOIs verified via Crossref (HTTP 200, matching titles/venues/authors with Nussbaum present): recent_1 (10.1016/j.apergo.2026.104831, Applied Ergonomics), recent_2 (10.1016/j.apergo.2026.104819, Applied Ergonomics), recent_3 (10.1016/j.apergo.2026.104796, Applied Ergonomics), seminal_1 (10.1111/j.1365-2648.2010.05597.x, Journal of Advanced Nursing 2011), seminal_2 (10.1016/j.gaitpost.2008.01.005, Gait & Posture 2008). Citation counts from Google Scholar, retrieved 2026-06-17 (seminal_1 ~657; seminal_2 ~630). To avoid duplicating the shared 2026 arm-support exoskeleton Human Factors paper that also serves as the lead recent item for co-author Sunwook Kim (index 56), distinct recent papers (lead authors Rajabi, Akinwande, Choi) were selected; the hf_papers remain valid candidates. No unverifiable citations are included in the output.
